secrets.name: Secret blocking (TruffleHog + Gitleaks pre-commit/pre-push)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(d) (protection against unauthorised access)
Annex I Part II §3 (effective and regular tests and reviews)
secrets.notes: Blocking credentials at commit/push keeps secrets out of the repo, closing the unauthorised-access vector that leaked credentials create.

commit-signing.name: Hardware-backed human-only commit signing + approved signers + human-signed merges
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of code, commands and configuration)
commit-signing.notes: Hardware-backed signatures cryptographically authenticate the human actor behind every commit and merge, making repository history tamper-evident.

agent-signing.name: AI-agent commit signing (distinct identity; never valid on protected branches)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of code, commands and configuration)
agent-signing.notes: Gives an AI agent a verifiable non-human signing identity so its commits are attributable and non-repudiable on feature branches, while the human-only protected-branch gate remains intact — humans, CI, and AI never share a key. Off by default.

branch-protection.name: GitHub branch protection verification
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of code, commands and configuration)
branch-protection.notes: Verified branch protection is the enforced technical control on protected named references that SLSA Source L3 requires and PS.1.1 code protection expects.

actions-audit.name: Actions SHA pinning + least-privilege permissions audit
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(j) (minimise attack surfaces)
actions-audit.notes: SHA-pinned actions and least-privilege tokens remove mutable-tag substitution and token abuse as external influences on the build (tj-actions CVE-2025-30066 class).

ai-trailers.name: Standardized AI commit trailers validation
ai-trailers.notes: Validated AI trailers are contemporaneous evidence of how each revision was produced — source provenance for AI-assisted changes.

ai-dep-gate.name: Extra gating for AI-introduced dependencies and shell commands
cra: Article 13(5) (due diligence on third-party components)
ai-dep-gate.notes: AI-suggested dependencies and shell commands are treated as untrusted input and receive heightened human review before acquisition (slopsquatting defense).

pr-template.name: AI-provenance PR template
pr-template.notes: The template forces AI-involvement disclosure so reviewers can scope review depth, and documents the project's contribution requirements.

ai-receipts.name: Cryptographic AI provenance receipts (optional)
ai-receipts.notes: Signed receipts upgrade AI-involvement claims from honor-system trailers to cryptographically verifiable attestations.

sbom.name: Syft CycloneDX SBOM generation
cra: Annex I Part II §1 (identify and document components incl. SBOM)
sbom.notes: The CycloneDX SBOM is the machine-readable component inventory CRA Annex I Part II §1 mandates and the component provenance data PS.3.2 describes.

vuln-scan.name: Trivy + OSV-Scanner vulnerability scanning
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(a) (no known exploitable vulnerabilities)
Annex I Part II §2 (address vulnerabilities without delay)
vuln-scan.notes: Continuous scanning identifies known-vulnerable components so they are fixed before release and remediated promptly afterward.

scorecard.name: OpenSSF Scorecard workflow
cra: Annex I Part II §3 (effective and regular tests and reviews)
scorecard.notes: Scheduled Scorecard runs are a recurring, evidence-producing review of the repository's supply-chain security posture.

renovate.name: Automated dependency updates (Renovate)
cra: Annex I Part II §2 (address vulnerabilities without delay)
renovate.notes: Automated update PRs shrink the window between an upstream security fix and its adoption in this project.

package-trust.name: Package trust (existence validation, new-package approval, typosquat heuristics, lockfile-exact installs)
cra: Article 13(5) (due diligence on third-party components)
package-trust.notes: Components are vetted before acquisition and installs are pinned exactly, defending against typosquatting, dependency confusion, and hallucinated package names.

grype.name: SBOM-first vulnerability scan (Grype, optional)
cra: Annex I Part II §1 (identify and document components incl. SBOM)
Annex I Part II §2 (address vulnerabilities without delay)
grype.notes: Scanning the CycloneDX SBOM itself makes the documented component inventory the vulnerability-detection surface.

socket-firewall.name: Malicious-package install firewall (Socket, optional)
cra: Article 13(5) (due diligence on third-party components)
socket-firewall.notes: Install-time firewall blocks known-malicious packages before they enter the environment (Shai-Hulud/lottie-player class).

sigstore-signing.name: Cosign keyless artifact signing + SBOM attestation
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of software)
Annex I Part II §7 (secure distribution of updates)
sigstore-signing.notes: Keyless signatures and SBOM attestations give acquirers cryptographic integrity-verification material for every released artifact.

slsa-provenance.name: SLSA Build L3 provenance (slsa-github-generator)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of software)
slsa-provenance.notes: The reusable trusted builder runs hosted and isolated, and its control plane signs provenance that build steps cannot forge — the Build L3 requirement set.

github-attestations.name: GitHub-native artifact attestations (actions/attest-build-provenance)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of software)
github-attestations.notes: A third, independent provenance trail in GitHub's own attestation store, verifiable anywhere with `gh attestation verify` and no extra tooling. Additive to the Cosign and slsa-github-generator trails; the Build L3 claim stays with the isolated trusted builder.

sbom-attestation.name: GitHub-native SBOM attestation (actions/attest, sbom-path)
cra: Annex I Part II(1) (software bill of materials in a machine-readable format)
sbom-attestation.notes: Binds the artifact's SBOM to its digest as a signed attestation in GitHub's own store, verifiable with `gh attestation verify` and no extra tooling. Deliberately NOT mapped to SLSA — SLSA Build levels cover provenance, not the SBOM predicate; SSDF PS.3.2 and CRA Annex I Part II(1) are the SBOM obligations this satisfies. Uses actions/attest in SBOM mode because actions/attest-sbom is deprecated.

provenance-verify.name: Provenance verification gates (slsa-verifier + cosign) before deploy/publish
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of software)
Annex I Part II §7 (secure distribution of updates)
provenance-verify.notes: Consumer-side verification per SLSA 'Verifying artifacts': nothing deploys or publishes unless signature and provenance validate, which is what gives Build-track claims teeth.

release-immutability.name: Immutable releases via draft-then-publish (release.yml)
cra: Annex I Part II §7 (secure distribution of updates)
release-immutability.notes: Every asset attaches to a DRAFT and the release publishes ONCE, so nothing is modified after it becomes immutable (GitHub release immutability). Provenance + SBOM go to the attestation store (immutability-safe). Trade-off: slsa-github-generator L3 provenance attaches to the release, which conflicts with immutability — use release-slsa.yml (L3, mutable) OR this (immutable, store-based). Opt-in; the setting itself is UI-only (no REST API).

octo-sts.name: Short-lived GitHub credential federation (octo-sts)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(d) (protection against unauthorised access)
octo-sts.notes: Federated short-lived tokens replace long-lived PATs, limiting the blast radius of CI credential theft to minutes.

harden-runner.name: CI runner egress/tamper monitoring (Harden-Runner)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of software)
harden-runner.notes: Egress filtering and file-tamper detection on runners keep unintended external influence out of builds (Ultralytics cache-poisoning class).

witness.name: in-toto witness attestations (optional)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of software)
witness.notes: Optional step-level in-toto attestations extend signed, verifiable provenance from the whole build down to individual pipeline steps.

sast.name: SAST (OpenGrep/Semgrep, local + CI)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(a) (no known exploitable vulnerabilities)
Annex I Part II §3 (effective and regular tests and reviews)
sast.notes: Static analysis in pre-commit and CI finds weaknesses in human-readable code before they ship.

sighthound.name: Fast local SAST (Sighthound, optional)
cra: Annex I Part II §3 (effective and regular tests and reviews)
sighthound.notes: Sub-second local SAST gives developers findings before commit; complements rather than replaces CI analysis.

codeql.name: Deep CI SAST (CodeQL)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(a) (no known exploitable vulnerabilities)
Annex I Part II §3 (effective and regular tests and reviews)
codeql.notes: Dataflow/taint analysis catches injection-class vulnerabilities that lightweight pattern-based SAST misses.

fuzzing.name: Continuous fuzzing (cargo-fuzz + ClusterFuzzLite)
cra: Annex I Part II §3 (effective and regular tests and reviews)
fuzzing.notes: Fuzz targets exercise the untrusted-input parsers (commit trailers, signer policy, dependency manifests) on every PR; ClusterFuzzLite is the integration OpenSSF Scorecard detects for Rust. Opt-in — needs project-specific fuzz targets in fuzz/.

workflow-audit-extended.name: Extended workflow audit (pull_request_target misuse, credential persistence, secret echo, risky-action substitution)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(j) (minimise attack surfaces)
workflow-audit-extended.notes: Audits the known CI takeover paths that let untrusted code or persisted credentials influence builds and exfiltrate secrets.

secure-repo.name: StepSecurity secure-repo onboarding
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(j) (minimise attack surfaces)
secure-repo.notes: Automated onboarding applies workflow hardening (pinning, least privilege, harden-runner); the specific guarantees are carried by those individual controls.

wait-for-secrets.name: Human-in-the-loop secret injection (wait-for-secrets, optional)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(d) (protection against unauthorised access)
wait-for-secrets.notes: High-value secrets are supplied by a human at run time instead of being stored in CI configuration at all.

dependency-track.name: Dependency-Track SBOM management platform integration
cra: Annex I Part II §1 (identify and document components incl. SBOM)
Annex I Part II §2 (address vulnerabilities without delay)
dependency-track.notes: Continuous analysis of stored SBOMs surfaces newly published CVEs against already-shipped component inventories.

guac.name: GUAC supply-chain knowledge graph ingestion
cra: Annex I Part II §1 (identify and document components incl. SBOM)
guac.notes: A queryable graph over SBOMs and attestations answers blast-radius questions (which artifacts contain component X) during vulnerability response.

openvex.name: OpenVEX generation/ingestion
cra: Annex I Part II §2 (address vulnerabilities without delay)
Annex I Part II §6 (facilitate information sharing on vulnerabilities)
openvex.notes: VEX records exploitability triage decisions machine-readably so scanners and consumers can act on not_affected status instead of re-triaging.

oras.name: OCI metadata storage via ORAS (optional)
cra: Annex I Part II §1 (identify and document components incl. SBOM)
oras.notes: Pushing SBOMs, signatures, and attestations to the registry co-locates verification material with the artifact consumers already pull.

compliance-map.name: Compliance map + sscsb report
cra: Article 13 (manufacturer obligations — documented conformity evidence)
compliance-map.notes: This map plus `sscsb report` turns enabled controls into auditable, machine-readable framework-coverage evidence.

gittuf.name: gittuf — signed, forge-independent policy over refs (RSL)
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of code, commands and configuration)
gittuf.notes: gittuf verifies who may change which refs cryptographically and independently of GitHub, extending protected-named-reference guarantees below the forge.

model-signing.name: OpenSSF Model Signing (Sigstore keyless) for ML model artifacts
cra: Annex I Part I(2)(f) (integrity of code, commands and configuration)
model-signing.notes: Signs ML model files with Sigstore and records to the transparency log so model provenance is verifiable and tampering detectable — the artifact-signing story extended to models.

security-insights.name: OpenSSF Security Insights (security-insights.yml)
cra: Article 13 (manufacturer obligations — documented conformity evidence)
security-insights.notes: Machine-readable declaration of the project's security practices and vulnerability-reporting channels, consumable by downstream tooling.

best-practices-badge.name: OpenSSF Best Practices Badge worksheet + evidence
best-practices-badge.notes: Pre-fills the OpenSSF Best Practices passing criteria from installed controls so earning the badge (and lifting Scorecard's CII check) is a copy-paste.

osps-baseline.name: OSPS Baseline assessment + report column
cra: Article 13 (manufacturer obligations — documented conformity evidence)
osps-baseline.notes: Maps enabled sscsb controls to OpenSSF Project Security Baseline requirement families (OSPS-AC/BR/QA/SA/VM) and adds an OSPS column to `sscsb report`.
